text: The surgeon places three 12 mm ports and two 5 mm ports in a standard configuration for laparoscopic access. The lesion is visually identified in the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon begins the procedure by moving the omentum away from the surgical field and positioning the bowel on the right side. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified near the duodenal flexure, serving as a landmark for initiating medial-to-lateral dissection along the avascular plane between the mesocolic fascia and Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the pancreas, fully dissecting and dividing the inferior mesenteric vein with an energy device after applying hemolytic clips. Assisted by visual aids, the inferior mesenteric artery is identified along with its branches, the left colic artery and the superior rectal artery.
The surgeon stretches the sigmoid colon to reveal the groove formed by the peritoneal fold, then makes peritoneal cuts to identify the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to identify and carefully separate the nerves from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), demonstrating the dissection plane between these structures. The dissection continues below the IMA to drop down the ureter and gonadal vessels, ensuring their preservation.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its origin, revealing the superior rectal artery and the left colic artery. Fibrofatty tissue and lymph nodes are excised along with the specimen to ensure complete mesocolic excision.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, entering the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The nerves are visible on either side of the dissection area within the presacral space.

The surgeon initiates lateral mobilization of the left colon along the line of Toldt, progressing towards the splenic flexure. Subsequently, the lesser sac is opened to facilitate further mobilization of the colon.

The surgeon identifies the left colic artery, which is then dissected, clipped, and divided. Care is taken to preserve the superior rectal artery to maintain vascularity to the rectal pouch, particularly as the specimen is divided at the level of the promontory.
The surgeon cuts the mesocolon at the distal margin, carefully isolating individual sigmoid branches. Once the distal margin is fully freed, it is divided using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon isolates and ligates individual sigmoid branches to prevent de-vascularization of the rectal pouch. The superior rectal artery is carefully preserved and dropped down. The specimen is delivered through an incision in the left iliac fossa, ensuring adequate margins.

The surgeon reintroduces the specimen into the peritoneal cavity and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler. The colon's orientation is confirmed visually to prevent twisting before completing the anastomosis.
